variables.P_loss: Power lost as heat in lines (W)

locked_facts: Power loss in transmission lines: P_loss = I²R
Since P = VI, current I = P/V — higher voltage means lower current for same power
Power loss in terms of voltage: P_loss = P²R/V² — loss is inversely proportional to V²
Step-up transformer increases voltage (reduces current) for long-distance transmission
Step-down transformer decreases voltage (increases current) for safe domestic use
Transformer relation: V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁ = I₁/I₂ (ideal transformer, no losses)
India: generated at ~11 kV, stepped up to 132–400 kV for transmission, stepped down to 220 V for homes
truth_statements: Transmitting power at high voltage drastically reduces I²R losses because current is inversely proportional to voltage
Doubling the transmission voltage reduces power loss by a factor of 4 (since P_loss ∝ 1/V²)
Step-up transformers are essential at the generating station to raise voltage before long-distance transmission
An ideal transformer conserves power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂ (input power = output power)
The resistance R of long transmission lines is fixed by the wire material and length — the only controllable factor is V

aha_moment.what_to_show: Same 100 kW power transmitted at 1000 V vs 100,000 V. At 1000 V: I = 100 A, P_loss = 100² × R = huge (glowing red wires, heat dissipation). At 100,000 V: I = 1 A, P_loss = 1² × R = tiny (cool wires). The 100× voltage increase gives 10,000× reduction in losses.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two transmission lines side by side: low voltage line glowing red hot with massive power loss bar, high voltage line cool with tiny loss bar. Same power delivered at destination.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that high voltage is NOT dangerous for transmission — it is ESSENTIAL. The danger is only at the consumer end, which is why step-down transformers exist.

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Power station generating P watts. Transmission line with resistance R. Destination load. No transformer — voltage is generation voltage (~11 kV). Current I = P/V calculated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Without step-up transformer: current is high for the required power. P_loss = I²R will be significant.

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Power transmitted at low voltage. Wires heat up (I²R loss). Large fraction of generated power wasted. Power delivered to load = P - I²R. Efficiency is poor. Wire temperature rises visually.
simulation_states.emphasis: At low voltage, current is large → I²R loss is enormous. Most power wasted as heat in wires.

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Step-up transformer added: V increased 100×, I decreased 100×. Same power P = VI. Now P_loss = I²R is 10,000× smaller. Wires are cool. Almost all power reaches the load. Step-down at destination for 220 V.
simulation_states.emphasis: 100× voltage up → 100× current down → 10,000× less loss. Same power, same wires, vastly different efficiency.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust transmission voltage, line resistance, and power to transmit. See P_loss, efficiency, and wire temperature update. Compare different voltages side by side. Calculate optimal transmission voltage.
simulation_states.emphasis: Raise voltage to see losses plummet. Try V = P/I at different levels. Why not infinitely high? (insulation breakdown, cost).

common_misconceptions.belief: High voltage transmission is dangerous so we should transmit at low voltage
trigger_phrases: low voltage pe bhejo safe hai
high voltage kyu use karte hain
common_misconceptions.correction: High voltage at the transmission stage is essential to minimize I²R losses. The danger is managed by using insulated towers and keeping lines far from ground. At the consumer end, voltage is stepped down to safe 220 V. Without high-voltage transmission, most generated power would be wasted as heat.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same power at 220 V vs 220 kV: at 220 V, 99% power lost in wires. At 220 kV, < 1% lost.

common_misconceptions.belief: Thicker wires solve the power loss problem, so transformers are unnecessary
trigger_phrases: wire badi karo transformer ki zaroorat nahi
common_misconceptions.correction: Thicker wires reduce R, but the cost and weight of copper/aluminum for hundreds of kilometers would be astronomical. Stepping up voltage is far more efficient and economical. Reducing I by 100× (via transformer) cuts loss by 10,000× — no practical wire thickness can match that.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Compare: 10× thicker wire (R/10) vs 10× higher voltage (P_loss/100). Voltage wins by 10×, and costs less.

legend.meaning: Generator producing P watts at V_gen
Increases voltage, decreases current for transmission
Long wire with resistance R (source of I²R loss)
Reduces voltage to safe level for consumers
Indicates heat dissipation from I²R losses
